news_summary: Today’s environmental news highlights successful species recovery, innovative zero-waste practices, bipartisan support for effective climate solutions, and promising safe carbon removal technology.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant la réussite de la restauration d'espèces, des pratiques innovantes en matière de zéro déchet, un soutien bipartisan en faveur de solutions climatiques efficaces, ainsi qu'une technologie prometteuse et sûre de piégeage du carbone.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan la recuperación exitosa de especies, prácticas innovadoras de cero residuos, el apoyo bipartidista a soluciones climáticas eficaces y una prometedora tecnología segura de eliminación de carbono.

articles.title: Rare plant makes comeback from brink of extinction
articles.summary: The Kentish milkwort has seen a seven-fold increase since conservation efforts in 2013.
articles.content: Next steps for the project will focus on maintaining the open conditions the species depends on, including the use of pigs at Queendown Warren to create and sustain areas of disturbed ground.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant positive impact on conservation with a rare plant species, the Kentish milkwort, making a substantial recovery due to targeted efforts since 2013. The story highlights ongoing ecological management practices that benefit biodiversity, which has broad environmental significance and clear substance.
articles.personality_title: Kentish milkwort plant bounces back after years of conservation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Kentish milkwort is a rare plant that was once very close to disappearing forever. It needs open, disturbed ground to grow well, which is not always easy to maintain naturally.

**What happened** – Since 2013, conservationists have worked hard to protect and help the Kentish milkwort. Their efforts have led to the plant’s numbers increasing seven times more than before.

**Impact** – This recovery shows that careful management of the land can save rare plants. Using pigs to create patches of disturbed soil is a unique way to keep the environment suitable for the Kentish milkwort and other wildlife.

**What's next step** – The project will continue using pigs at Queendown Warren to keep the ground open and healthy for the plant. This ongoing work aims to support the milkwort’s growth and protect the area’s biodiversity.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Targeted conservation and smart land management have helped the Kentish milkwort recover from near extinction.
articles.personality_title_fr: La plante Kentish milkwort renaît après des années de conservation
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Kentish milkwort est une plante rare qui était presque disparue. Elle a besoin de terrains ouverts et perturbés pour bien pousser, ce qui n’est pas toujours facile à maintenir naturellement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Depuis 2013, les conservateurs ont travaillé dur pour protéger et aider le Kentish milkwort. Le nombre de plantes a augmenté de sept fois grâce à ces efforts.

**Impact** – Cette reprise montre qu’une gestion attentive des terres peut sauver des plantes rares. Utiliser des cochons pour créer des zones de sol perturbé est une méthode originale pour garder un environnement adapté au Kentish milkwort et à d’autres espèces.

**Prochaine étape** – Le projet continuera à utiliser des cochons à Queendown Warren pour maintenir le sol ouvert et sain pour la plante. Ce travail vise à soutenir la croissance du milkwort et à protéger la biodiversité locale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La conservation ciblée et une gestion intelligente des terres ont permis au Kentish milkwort de se rétablir après avoir frôlé l’extinction.
articles.personality_title_es: La planta Kentish milkwort se recupera tras años de conservación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Kentish milkwort es una planta rara que estuvo muy cerca de desaparecer. Necesita terrenos abiertos y perturbados para crecer bien, lo cual no siempre es fácil mantener de forma natural.

**Qué pasó** – Desde 2013, los conservacionistas han trabajado duro para proteger y ayudar al Kentish milkwort. Gracias a estos esfuerzos, la cantidad de plantas aumentó siete veces.

**Impacto** – Esta recuperación muestra que una gestión cuidadosa del terreno puede salvar plantas raras. Usar cerdos para crear áreas de suelo perturbado es una forma original de mantener un ambiente adecuado para el Kentish milkwort y otras especies.

**Próximo paso** – El proyecto seguirá usando cerdos en Queendown Warren para mantener el suelo abierto y saludable para la planta. Este trabajo continuo busca apoyar el crecimiento del milkwort y proteger la biodiversidad del área.

**Resumen en una frase** – La conservación dirigida y una gestión inteligente del terreno han ayudado al Kentish milkwort a recuperarse tras estar al borde de la extinción.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a rare, delicate plant thriving in a sunlit clearing of Queendown Warren, with content pigs gently rooting in the earth nearby, creating patches of freshly disturbed soil that surround the vibrant plant, all rendered in natural, soft earth tones and greens.

articles.title: Zero-Waste Cleaning and Laundry Tips
articles.summary:
One load of laundry can release up to 1.5 million tiny plastic fibers into the...
The post Zero-Waste Cleaning and Laundry Tips appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
One load of laundry can release up to 1.5 million tiny plastic fibers into the water that drains out of your washing machine. Most water treatment plants can’t catch fibers that small, so they end up in rivers, lakes, and the ocean. Scientists now think laundry is responsible for about 35% of the small plastic pieces found in the sea.

That changes what “zero-waste” cleaning actually means today. The plastic detergent bottle is the obvious problem. The hidden problems, including shedding fibers, plastic films sold as “eco-friendly,” mystery fragrance chemicals, and contaminants you’ll never see on a label, are the bigger concern. But here’s the good news: most of the simple ingredients people have used for generations still work, and a few small upgrades make the rest of your routine a lot cleaner.

Cleaning Your Home

Most chemicals in store-bought cleaners haven’t been fully tested for long-term health effects. The EPA’s Safer Choice program certifies products made without ingredients linked to cancer, hormone problems, or harm to wildlife. About 2,000 products carry the label. Almost lost in a 2025 budget cut, the program survived but with fewer staff. Words like “natural” and “green” on packaging aren’t regulated and don’t really mean anything, so look for the Safer Choice label or check the EWG Guide to Healthy Cleaning before trusting a brand.

Making your own cleaners gives you control, cuts packaging, and saves money. The basic kit is short: baking soda for scrubbing, white vinegar for windows and mineral stains, lemon juice for cutting boards, 3% hydrogen peroxide (in a dark bottle) for stains and germs, and castile soap for general cleaning. A spray bottle of half vinegar, half water cleans most surfaces. Reuse jars and spray bottles instead of buying new ones.

One important update: older recipes, including earlier versions of this article, used borax as a staple ingredient. Newer research has changed that advice. Europe added borax to its list of substances of very high concern in 2010 because high doses caused reproductive problems in animals, and California lists it as a reproductive toxin under Proposition 65. Borax isn’t banned in the U.S., but the Environmental Working Group recommends skipping it in homemade cleaners. Plenty of borax-free recipes work just as well.

About killing germs: the popular advice to spray vinegar, then hydrogen peroxide, came from a 1996 study on beef tissue, not on home surfaces. Vinegar at normal household strength doesn’t reliably kill many germs, including norovirus and several drug-resistant bacteria, and it isn’t EPA-registered as a disinfectant. For everyday cleaning, vinegar is fine. When real germ-killing matters, when cleaning up after handling raw meat or during a stomach flu outbreak, use 3% hydrogen peroxide alone or an EPA-registered disinfectant.

Never mix peroxide and vinegar in the same bottle and don’t mix bleach with vinegar or any acid; the gases created when these are mixed is dangerous.

Laundry

The laundry room in a great place to start your zero-waste journey.

Microfibers. Synthetic fabrics like polyester, nylon, and fleece shed tiny plastic threads every time you wash them. France passed a law requiring built-in filters on all new washing machines, which took effect January 1, 2025. California passed a similar law in 2023, but the governor vetoed it. Oregon, New York, and several other states have filter bills moving through their legislatures. Until U.S. machines come with filters, you can use a microfiber-catching laundry bag like Guppyfriend or a Cora Ball, or attach an external filter from Filtrol or PlanetCare to your drain hose. These catch up to 90% of fibers.

“Plastic-free” laundry sheets and pods. Most laundry sheets use a film made from polyvinyl alcohol (PVA or PVOH), which dissolves in water. The cleaning industry says PVA breaks down completely in wastewater treatment, but a 2021 study estimated that about 75% of it passes through treatment plants intact and persists in the environment. The science is debated, but the labels aren’t: if you see polyvinyl alcohol, PVOH, or PVA on the package, the dissolving film is a synthetic plastic. Powdered detergent in cardboard, concentrated liquid in glass, or PVA-free sheet brands are alternatives that avoid this question.

A hidden carcinogen called 1,4-dioxane. This chemical isn’t added to detergent on purpose — it’s a leftover from how certain ingredients are made. Because it’s a contaminant rather than an ingredient, manufacturers don’t have to list it. Independent testing has found it in most conventional detergents. New York finalized rules in September 2024 limiting it to 1 part per million, and the EPA officially called it an unreasonable health risk in November 2024. To avoid it, skip detergents listing SLES (sodium laureth sulfate), “PEG” anything, or ingredients with “-eth-” in the name.

Skip dryer sheets. A University of Washington study found dryer vents emit more than 25 different volatile chemicals when scented detergent and dryer sheets are used together. Seven are classified as hazardous air pollutants. Wool dryer balls reduce drying time and static without coating clothes in chemicals. For scent, put a few drops of essential oil on a damp washcloth and toss it in.

Wash cold. About 90% of the energy a washing machine uses goes to heating water. Switching from warm to cold cycles saves about 3.2 kWh per load, roughly the same as running your fridge for 10 months over a year’s worth of laundry. Cold water also makes clothes last longer and shed fewer microfibers. Modern detergents are designed to clean in cold water. Replace fabric softener with half a cup of white vinegar in the rinse cycle. If you’re shopping for a new dryer, heat-pump dryers use 20–60% less energy than conventional ones.

What You Can Do Today

Wash in cold water on shorter cycles. Saves energy, money, and reduces microfiber shedding.

Use a microfiber-catching laundry bag, ball, or external filter.

Skip dryer sheets and fabric softener. Use wool dryer balls and vinegar instead.

Read ingredient lists. Avoid SLES and PEG compounds in detergent. Skip products with PVA in their dissolvable film if microplastics matter to you.

Make your own cleaners with baking soda, vinegar, peroxide, and castile soap. Skip borax.

Look for the EPA Safer Choice label on store-bought products.

Never mix bleach with vinegar or any other acid.

Support state and federal microfiber filter laws so this stops being a consumer-level problem.

Related Reading

Featured image by Monfocus from Pixabay

Editor’s note: Originally authored by Sarah Lozanova on May 18, 2016, this article was substantially updated in May 2026.
articles.reasoning: The article provides detailed, actionable advice on reducing plastic microfiber pollution and harmful chemicals in household cleaning and laundry, highlighting recent legislative progress and practical solutions with broad environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: Simple steps to reduce plastic pollution from laundry and cleaning
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Washing clothes can release tiny plastic fibers into water, which most treatment plants cannot remove. These fibers end up in rivers and oceans, making laundry a big source of plastic pollution. Cleaning products can also contain harmful chemicals that affect health and the environment.

**What happened** – New laws in France and some US states are requiring washing machines to have filters that catch these plastic fibers. Meanwhile, experts suggest using microfiber-catching laundry bags or external filters to reduce pollution. They also recommend homemade cleaners using safe ingredients like baking soda and vinegar, and avoiding products with hidden harmful chemicals.

**Impact** – These changes help reduce millions of tiny plastics entering water systems and avoid exposure to unsafe chemicals in cleaning products. Using cold water saves energy and keeps clothes lasting longer. Switching to safer products and methods makes everyday cleaning healthier for people and the environment.

**What's next step** – More states may pass laws to require fiber filters on washing machines. Consumers can choose safer products, make their own cleaners, and support these laws. Research and testing will continue to improve cleaning safety and reduce pollution.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using simple tools and safer cleaning habits can cut plastic pollution and harmful chemicals from laundry and home cleaning.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des gestes simples pour réduire la pollution plastique liée à la lessive et au nettoyage
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Laver ses vêtements peut libérer de minuscules fibres plastiques dans l'eau, que la plupart des stations d'épuration ne peuvent pas filtrer. Ces fibres finissent dans les rivières et les océans, faisant de la lessive une source importante de pollution plastique. Les produits de nettoyage contiennent aussi parfois des substances nocives pour la santé et l'environnement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – De nouvelles lois en France et dans certains États américains obligent les machines à laver à être équipées de filtres pour retenir ces fibres plastiques. En attendant, les experts recommandent d’utiliser des sacs ou filtres spéciaux pour capturer les microfibres. Ils conseillent aussi de fabriquer ses propres produits nettoyants avec des ingrédients sûrs comme le bicarbonate de soude et le vinaigre, et d’éviter les produits contenant des substances nocives cachées.

**Impact** – Ces mesures permettent de réduire des millions de petites fibres plastiques dans l’eau et d’éviter l’exposition à des produits chimiques dangereux. Utiliser de l’eau froide économise de l’énergie et prolonge la durée de vie des vêtements. Adopter des produits et méthodes plus sûrs rend le nettoyage quotidien plus sain pour les personnes et la planète.

**Prochaine étape** – D’autres États pourraient adopter des lois pour rendre obligatoires les filtres sur les machines à laver. Les consommateurs peuvent choisir des produits plus sûrs, fabriquer leurs nettoyants et soutenir ces lois. Les recherches continueront pour améliorer la sécurité des produits et réduire la pollution.

**Phrase clé** – De simples outils et habitudes de nettoyage plus sûres réduisent la pollution plastique et les produits chimiques nocifs liés à la lessive et au nettoyage.
articles.personality_title_es: Pasos sencillos para reducir la contaminación plástica de la lavandería y limpieza
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Lavar la ropa puede liberar pequeñas fibras plásticas en el agua, que la mayoría de las plantas de tratamiento no pueden eliminar. Estas fibras terminan en ríos y océanos, convirtiendo la lavandería en una gran fuente de contaminación plástica. Los productos de limpieza también pueden contener químicos dañinos para la salud y el medio ambiente.

**Qué pasó** – Nuevas leyes en Francia y algunos estados de EE.UU. exigen que las lavadoras tengan filtros que atrapen estas fibras plásticas. Mientras tanto, expertos recomiendan usar bolsas o filtros especiales para capturar microfibras. También sugieren hacer limpiadores caseros con ingredientes seguros como bicarbonato y vinagre, y evitar productos con químicos ocultos dañinos.

**Impacto** – Estos cambios ayudan a reducir millones de plásticos diminutos que llegan al agua y evitan la exposición a químicos inseguros. Usar agua fría ahorra energía y hace que la ropa dure más. Cambiar a productos y métodos más seguros hace que la limpieza diaria sea más saludable para las personas y el ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Más estados podrían aprobar leyes para exigir filtros en las lavadoras. Los consumidores pueden elegir productos más seguros, hacer sus propios limpiadores y apoyar estas leyes. La investigación continuará para mejorar la seguridad y reducir la contaminación.

**Frase clave** – Usar herramientas simples y hábitos de limpieza más seguros puede reducir la contaminación plástica y los químicos dañinos de la lavandería y limpieza.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cozy laundry room scene featuring a gently flowing stream beside it where tiny, delicate fibers are caught by a translucent mesh laundry bag and a round microfiber-catching ball, with natural cleaning ingredients like a jar of baking soda, a bottle of vinegar, and a bar of castile soap neatly arranged on a wooden shelf, all rendered in soft, earthy tones and simple colors.

articles.title: The surprising climate fix that Democrats and Republicans both love
articles.content:
Democrats and Republicans agree on virtually nothing at this point, except the desperate need to build more housing in the United States. Depending on your viewpoint, the country needs new domiciles because it puts people to work and stimulates local economies, or because it creates affordable homes and drives down housing costs, thus reducing homelessness. Affordability, including in housing, is now one of the biggest political issues in America.

Neither party, though, is talking about the secret superpower of new apartment buildings: They’re much better for the planet than constructing single-family homes. According to a new report, these units are “an almost automatic form of building decarbonization,” because three-quarters of new apartments are heated electrically. That means they can run on rooftop solar panels or tap into grids humming with clean energy, instead of burning plant-warming natural gas in furnaces or boilers.

While the Trump administration and the Republican Party at large try to roll back as much climate progress as they can, they’re inadvertently bolstering that progress by calling for new construction. Deep-red Montana, for instance, recently passed a flurry of bills to get more multi-family housing built. “Apartments are the climate solution hiding in plain sight,” said Alan Durning, executive director of the nonprofit Sightline Institute, which authored the report.

Nothing against single-family homes, but apartment buildings and condos are much more efficient for a number of reasons. For one, residents share walls, floors, and ceilings with their neighbors, surrounding them with excellent insulation. Secondly, the square footage of each unit tends to be smaller than detached homes, so there’s less air to manage. Accordingly, it takes less energy to climate-control apartment units and keep people comfortable: The typical resident of a downtown high-rise emits one-third as much greenhouse gases as a resident of a detached house in the suburbs.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Because of this inherent efficiency, apartment builders have for decades opted to install what’s called electric resistance heating, like baseboard heaters, instead of gas furnaces. That’s because wiring them up is cheaper than piping in all that methane. “If I am building something with the intention of renting it, I really want to minimize my upfront costs,” said Amanda D. Smith, senior scientist at the climate solutions nonprofit Project Drawdown, who studies the built environment but wasn’t involved in the new report. “Often electric water heaters and electric heaters for space heating make sense from that perspective.”

Economic forces, then, have long encouraged the adoption of such systems: 68 percent of apartments built since the early 1970s have been heated with electricity, the report notes. Half a century ago, no one was campaigning to decarbonize buildings to fight climate change — going electric was just the better option. Today, if you live in an apartment, you’re 60 percent more likely to be all-electric than your neighbor living in a house.

And apartments can get even greener. Heat pumps — which move warmth from outdoor air inside, instead of generating it like a gas furnace does — are around three times more efficient than space heaters. Over the past few decades, the technology has gotten more powerful, capable of extracting heat from even freezing outdoor air. That’s helped heat pumps proliferate across even the chilliest climes: Maine installed 100,000 of the appliances two years ahead of schedule, and almost two-thirds of households in Norway use them. Heat pumps are increasingly popping up in American apartment buildings, too: While quite rare in the decades after the 1950s, heat pumps have been incorporated into 18 percent of these structures in the Northwest since 2010, the report notes. (Overall, heat pumps have outsold gas furnaces in the U.S. for several years now.)

While traditional electric heat pumps work like air conditioners, in that you need an outdoor unit that connects to an indoor one, new varieties are easier to incorporate into apartments and condos. One from a company called Gradient fits like a saddle over a window sill and plugs into a regular outlet, with installation taking less than a half hour. (Think of it like those old-school AC units jutting out of city apartment windows, only much cooler looking.) Another launching this winter combines the two units into one attached to an interior wall, where it exchanges air with the outside. “Making retrofits simpler will be a game-changer,” Smith said.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

If new buildings in hotter parts of the U.S. rely upon gas heating, they’d still need an air conditioning system. The beauty of a heat pump is that it can reverse in the summer to fill a home with cool air. As temperatures rise across the country, heat pumps will not only work more efficiently than space heaters and gas furnaces to warm apartments, but to provide invaluable cooling to keep people healthy. Already in the U.S., heat kills more people every year than all other forms of extreme weather combined.

Making a building’s heating fully electric encourages the adoption of another appliance critical for reducing greenhouse gas emissions: the induction stove. “If you’re building a building and you’re heating and cooling with heat pumps, it doesn’t really make sense to hook it up to the gas system to pipe a tiny bit of gas in for people to cook on their gas stoves a couple of times a week,” said Matt Casale, managing director of states and regions at the nonprofit Building Decarbonization Coalition, which wasn’t involved in the report.

All this electrification could potentially slot into a burgeoning technology known as networked geothermal. Instead of a building’s heat pump using outdoor air, it uses liquid pumping underground. Because the earth’s temperature remains a more consistent temperature year-round than the air, these heat pumps are even more efficient at warming a space. If all of an area’s buildings — apartments or otherwise — are hooked into a networked geothermal system, there’s no need to pipe gas into the neighborhood at all. “It’s a real community-based energy system, and you’re using energy that’s literally homegrown,” Casale said. “It’s right under your feet.”

Beyond their superior energy efficiency and tendency to go electric, apartment buildings provide denser housing, fitting far more people into a footprint than a single-family home could manage. If located near daily essentials, like grocery stores, residents can walk instead of drive, further reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Ideally, robust public transportation systems can get those apartment-dwellers anywhere they can’t walk to.

Building big apartment buildings of just apartments, though, just won’t cut it, said Cécile Faraud, head of the clean construction program at C40, a global network of climate-focused mayors. These structures need mixed uses, where living spaces sit atop commercial spaces, like markets and doctors’ offices. “So you can access care, you can access education, you can access your needs in terms of shopping,” said Faraud, who wasn’t involved in the report. “But also in terms of health, so being able to exercise in parks, etc., and access to nature.”

Indeed, what surrounds these apartment complexes matters too. Green spaces reduce temperatures, boost residents’ mental health, and provide habitats for native plant and animal species. Better yet, “agrihoods” surround working farms with multi-family housing, generating nutritious produce for residents to enjoy or sell. (Faraud stresses that in addition to creating more housing, cities need to retrofit existing buildings to be more energy efficient, like with double-paned windows and better insulation.)

Constructing apartments, though, is often way more difficult than it should be, housing advocates say. The new report notes that “apartment buildings of at least four stories are currently allowed on less than 1 percent of the residential land in all but 10 Oregon cities” — even in progressive Portland, that figure is 14 percent. “The main thing that we need to do is re-legalize apartments in a much larger area of our cities,” Durning said.

Cities and states are responsible for that, not the feds. But the growing national push from both parties to get more units built will be a win-win for people and the planet. “Even across a political landscape that’s as fractured and divided and as contentious as what we’re seeing now,” Smith said, “I think most people are willing to say: We want people to have homes.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights bipartisan support for building more multi-family housing in the U.S., which has significant environmental benefits by reducing greenhouse gas emissions through electrification and energy efficiency. It provides detailed context on how apartments inherently reduce carbon footprints compared to single-family homes, discusses advances in heat pump technology, and emphasizes the broader climate and social benefits of denser, mixed-use housing. The story has broad societal and environmental impact, focusing on a meaningful climate solution with substantial positive real-world effects.
articles.personality_title: Building more apartments helps both people and the planet
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the United States, housing is a big problem. Many people want more affordable homes, and both Democrats and Republicans agree that building more housing is important. But few realize that apartment buildings can also help reduce pollution and fight climate change.

**What happened** – A new report shows that most new apartments use electric heating instead of gas. This means they can use clean energy from solar panels or electric grids instead of burning natural gas, which causes pollution. Apartments share walls and use less space, so they need less energy to heat and cool. Technologies like heat pumps, which are very energy-efficient, are becoming more common in apartments. Some new heat pumps are easy to install, even in older buildings.

**Impact** – Apartments produce much less greenhouse gas than single-family houses. People living in apartments create about one-third the pollution of those in detached homes. Building apartments near stores and parks helps people walk more and drive less, further cutting pollution. This makes apartments a hidden but powerful way to fight climate change while also providing affordable homes.

**What's next step** – Cities need to allow more apartment buildings to be built because right now, most places restrict them. Experts also say new apartments should include shops, parks, and other services nearby to improve quality of life. More buildings will use better technologies like heat pumps and electric stoves, and some may connect to underground heating systems that use the earth’s natural temperature.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Building more electric-heated apartments is a practical way to create affordable homes and reduce pollution at the same time.
articles.personality_title_fr: Construire plus d’appartements aide à la fois les gens et la planète
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aux États-Unis, le logement est un grand problème. Beaucoup de gens veulent des maisons abordables, et démocrates comme républicains sont d’accord pour dire qu’il faut construire plus de logements. Mais peu savent que les immeubles d’appartements peuvent aussi aider à réduire la pollution et à lutter contre le changement climatique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un nouveau rapport montre que la plupart des nouveaux appartements utilisent le chauffage électrique au lieu du gaz. Cela signifie qu’ils peuvent utiliser de l’énergie propre provenant de panneaux solaires ou des réseaux électriques, au lieu de brûler du gaz naturel, qui pollue. Les appartements partagent des murs et prennent moins de place, donc ils ont besoin de moins d’énergie pour chauffer et climatiser. Des technologies comme les pompes à chaleur, très efficaces, se répandent dans les appartements. Certaines pompes à chaleur sont faciles à installer, même dans les vieux bâtiments.

**Impact** – Les appartements produisent beaucoup moins de gaz à effet de serre que les maisons individuelles. Les habitants d’appartements émettent environ un tiers de la pollution des habitants de maisons isolées. Construire des appartements près des commerces et des parcs aide les gens à marcher plus et à conduire moins, ce qui réduit encore la pollution. Cela fait des appartements une solution discrète mais puissante pour lutter contre le changement climatique tout en offrant des logements abordables.

**Prochaine étape** – Les villes doivent autoriser la construction de plus d’immeubles d’appartements car aujourd’hui, la plupart des endroits les limitent. Les experts disent aussi que les nouveaux appartements devraient inclure des commerces, des parcs et d’autres services à proximité pour améliorer la vie des habitants. Plus de bâtiments utiliseront des technologies comme les pompes à chaleur et les cuisinières électriques, et certains pourraient être reliés à des systèmes de chauffage souterrains utilisant la température naturelle de la terre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Construire plus d’appartements chauffés à l’électricité est une façon pratique de créer des logements abordables tout en réduisant la pollution.
articles.personality_title_es: Construir más apartamentos ayuda tanto a las personas como al planeta
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Estados Unidos, la vivienda es un gran problema. Muchas personas quieren hogares asequibles, y tanto demócratas como republicanos están de acuerdo en que se deben construir más viviendas. Pero pocos saben que los edificios de apartamentos también pueden ayudar a reducir la contaminación y combatir el cambio climático.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo informe muestra que la mayoría de los apartamentos nuevos usan calefacción eléctrica en lugar de gas. Esto significa que pueden usar energía limpia de paneles solares o redes eléctricas, en lugar de quemar gas natural que contamina. Los apartamentos comparten paredes y ocupan menos espacio, por lo que necesitan menos energía para calentar y enfriar. Tecnologías como las bombas de calor, que son muy eficientes, se están usando más en los apartamentos. Algunas bombas de calor nuevas son fáciles de instalar, incluso en edificios antiguos.

**Impacto** – Los apartamentos producen mucho menos gases de efecto invernadero que las casas individuales. Las personas que viven en apartamentos generan alrededor de un tercio de la contaminación que generan quienes viven en casas separadas. Construir apartamentos cerca de tiendas y parques ayuda a que las personas caminen más y conduzcan menos, reduciendo aún más la contaminación. Esto hace que los apartamentos sean una solución poderosa pero poco conocida para combatir el cambio climático y ofrecer viviendas asequibles.

**Próximo paso** – Las ciudades necesitan permitir que se construyan más edificios de apartamentos porque ahora la mayoría los limita. Los expertos también dicen que los nuevos apartamentos deberían incluir tiendas, parques y otros servicios cerca para mejorar la calidad de vida. Más edificios usarán tecnologías como bombas de calor y estufas eléctricas, y algunos podrían conectarse a sistemas de calefacción subterráneos que usan la temperatura natural de la tierra.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Construir más apartamentos con calefacción eléctrica es una forma práctica de crear viviendas asequibles y reducir la contaminación al mismo tiempo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant, multi-story apartment building with rooftop solar panels glowing softly, surrounded by a lively mixed-use neighborhood featuring small markets and parks with community gardens, all bathed in natural, earthy tones that emphasize harmony between sustainable urban living and environmental care.

articles.title: First test of CO2 removal with green sand finds no harm to marine life
articles.summary: Adding olivine to the ocean could remove CO2 from the atmosphere, and a pilot project in New York state found no signs of adverse effects on seafloor organisms
articles.content:
Adding olivine to the ocean could remove CO2 from the atmosphere, and a pilot project in New York state found no signs of adverse effects on seafloor organisms

A beach in Southampton, New York, treated with olivine sand Chayenne Moreau

The first trial to absorb atmospheric carbon dioxide by adding crushed olivine to the ocean had no adverse effects on the seafloor ecosystem in its first year.

While the results of the trial in New York state are promising for this carbon-removal technique, researcherss warn they may not have captured all potential negative impacts.

The addition of olivine to the ocean should still be carefully regulated, “but there might be ways that it could work and have a minimal effect”, says Emilia Jankowska at the non-profit group Hourglass Climate, who led the study.

The United Nations climate body has said the world will need carbon removal methods, which range from planting trees to filtering out CO2 from the air with giant machines, to reach net-zero greenhouse gas emissions. As emissions continue to rise and the goal of limiting global warming to 1.5°C above preindustrial levels slips out of reach, many hope these technologies could someday help cool the planet back down.

Olivine, or magnesium iron silicate, is a greenish mineral common in Earth’s mantle. Once it comes to the surface, it tends to react with the CO2 dissolved in rainwater to form metals, silicates and bicarbonate, a stable compound that eventually flows into the ocean, locking CO2 away for thousands of years.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

Spreading crushed olivine and other silicates on farm fields could speed up this process and remove up to 1.1 billion tonnes of CO2 annually, recent research found. But US start-up Vesta plans to add olivine directly to the ocean, where it would turn dissolved CO2 into bicarbonate and allow the ocean to absorb more CO2 from the atmosphere.

Olivine typically contains trace amounts of heavy metals, however, and lab studies have found elevated levels of nickel and chromium in crustaceans and molluscs exposed to it. Researchers have also been concerned that the sand could smother bottom-dwellers like crustaceans, molluscs, snails and worms.

In 2022, Vesta deposited 650 tonnes of olivine sand along a beach in Long Island, New York state, on top of 13,500 tonnes of regular sand added to bolster the shore, which is being swept away as storms intensify. The tide and waves carried the olivine out to sea.

Researchers scooped up sediment from the shallows out to 160 metres offshore before and after the olivine addition and again a year later. They compared this to sediments near sections of the beach where only normal sand was added and where no sand was added.

Of dozens of species, only a tiny worm called the fringed blood worm significantly declined in the olivine area, and the overall abundance and diversity of bottom-dwelling species recovered within two months. While the species composition changed, it also changed in the area where only normal sand was added, which suggests the common practice of beach nourishment was to blame.

Most importantly, concentrations of nickel, chromium cobalt and manganese in the organisms remained low. “The natural system is just so dynamic that any dissolving constituents are very rapidly diluted,” says Jankowska.

Vesta conducted the environmental monitoring of the field trial, and Jankowska and the founder of Hourglass formerly worked for the company. But Hourglass received philanthropic funding from the Grantham Foundation to independently analyse the monitoring data.

Dissolving olivine in the ocean can encourage the calcium carbonate to precipitate out of seawater, locking away the trace metals, according to Christopher Pearce at the National Oceanography Centre in the UK. That can also cut down on how much additional CO2 the seawater can absorb, however.

“It’s a really critical study to move from lab-based understanding to real-world interactions,” he says. “Further trials… will be important for understanding different biological responses… and different rates of [CO2] uptake.”

But the study’s claim of no adverse effects is “stronger than what the evidence shows”, says James Kerry at the non-profit group OceanCare. Fluctuations of olivine concentrations due to what the study called “burial–re-exposure cycles” meant the olivine might have been buried much of the time under the far larger quantity of normal sand deposited, he says.

“The lack of accumulation that’s apparent may reflect limited exposure, not necessarily that the material is intrinsically safe,” he says.

Hourglass Climate is now monitoring the impacts of a larger trial in which Vesta sank 8200 tonnes of olivine 450 metres off the coast of Duck, North Carolina, in 2024. Preliminary results suggest species abundance and diversity recovered, while Hourglass is still analysing metal accumulation, according to Jankowska.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a pioneering pilot project testing olivine addition to oceans for CO2 removal, showing no significant harm to marine life, which represents a promising carbon removal technique with potential broad environmental benefits. The study provides detailed context about the trial, its environmental monitoring, and implications for climate action, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: Ocean trial shows adding olivine sand safely removes CO2 without harming sea life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are exploring ways to reduce carbon dioxide (CO2) in the atmosphere to fight climate change. One idea is to add a mineral called olivine to the ocean. Olivine reacts with CO2 dissolved in seawater, turning it into a stable form that stays locked away for thousands of years. But there were worries this might harm sea creatures living on the ocean floor.

**What happened** – In 2022, a company named Vesta spread 650 tonnes of crushed olivine sand on a beach in Long Island, New York. Researchers studied the ocean floor creatures before, during, and a year after the olivine was added. They compared areas with olivine, regular sand, and no added sand. They looked at many species and measured metals like nickel and chromium, which can be harmful if too high.

**Impact** – The study found no major harm to sea life. Only one small worm species slightly decreased, but the overall number and variety of animals bounced back quickly. Metal levels in creatures stayed low, probably because the ocean water spreads and dilutes minerals fast. This is the first real-world test of adding olivine to the ocean, showing it might be a safe way to remove CO2 from the air.

**What’s next step** – Scientists say more tests are needed to fully understand long-term effects and different ocean conditions. A bigger trial is happening off the coast of North Carolina, where 8,200 tonnes of olivine were placed underwater in 2024. Researchers will keep studying how sea life responds and how much CO2 is absorbed.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Early results from adding olivine sand to the ocean suggest it can help remove CO2 without hurting marine animals, but more research is needed to confirm its safety and effectiveness.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un essai en mer montre que le sable d’olivine élimine le CO2 sans nuire à la vie marine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent des moyens de réduire le dioxyde de carbone (CO2) dans l’atmosphère pour lutter contre le changement climatique. Une idée est d’ajouter un minéral appelé olivine dans l’océan. L’olivine réagit avec le CO2 dissous dans l’eau de mer, le transformant en une forme stable qui reste enfermée pendant des milliers d’années. Mais on craignait que cela ne nuise aux animaux vivant au fond de la mer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2022, une entreprise nommée Vesta a répandu 650 tonnes de sable d’olivine concassé sur une plage de Long Island, New York. Des chercheurs ont étudié les animaux du fond marin avant, pendant, et un an après l’ajout de l’olivine. Ils ont comparé des zones avec de l’olivine, du sable ordinaire, et sans sable ajouté. Ils ont observé de nombreuses espèces et mesuré des métaux comme le nickel et le chrome, qui peuvent être dangereux en trop grande quantité.

**Impact** – L’étude n’a pas trouvé de dommages importants pour la vie marine. Une seule petite espèce de ver a un peu diminué, mais le nombre et la diversité des animaux sont rapidement revenus à la normale. Les niveaux de métaux dans les organismes sont restés faibles, sûrement parce que l’eau de mer dilue vite ces minéraux. C’est la première expérience réelle d’ajout d’olivine dans l’océan, montrant que cela pourrait être une méthode sûre pour éliminer le CO2 de l’air.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques disent qu’il faut plus d’essais pour bien comprendre les effets à long terme et dans différents milieux marins. Un essai plus grand a lieu au large de la Caroline du Nord, où 8 200 tonnes d’olivine ont été déposées en 2024. Les chercheurs continueront à observer la vie marine et la quantité de CO2 absorbée.

**Phrase clé** – Les premiers résultats montrent que l’ajout de sable d’olivine dans l’océan pourrait aider à éliminer le CO2 sans nuire aux animaux marins, mais il faut encore confirmer sa sécurité et son efficacité.
articles.personality_title_es: Prueba en el océano muestra que la arena de olivino elimina CO2 sin dañar la vida marina
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan formas de reducir el dióxido de carbono (CO2) en la atmósfera para combatir el cambio climático. Una idea es añadir un mineral llamado olivino al océano. El olivino reacciona con el CO2 disuelto en el agua de mar, convirtiéndolo en una forma estable que se mantiene guardada por miles de años. Pero había preocupaciones de que esto pudiera dañar a los animales que viven en el fondo del mar.

**Qué pasó** – En 2022, una empresa llamada Vesta extendió 650 toneladas de arena triturada de olivino en una playa de Long Island, Nueva York. Los investigadores estudiaron a los animales del fondo marino antes, durante y un año después de añadir el olivino. Compararon áreas con olivino, con arena normal y sin arena añadida. Observaron muchas especies y midieron metales como níquel y cromo, que pueden ser dañinos si están en niveles altos.

**Impacto** – El estudio no encontró daños importantes a la vida marina. Solo una especie pequeña de gusano disminuyó un poco, pero la cantidad y variedad de animales se recuperó rápido. Los niveles de metales en los organismos se mantuvieron bajos, probablemente porque el agua del océano dispersa y diluye rápido estos minerales. Esta es la primera prueba real de añadir olivino al océano, mostrando que podría ser una forma segura de eliminar CO2 del aire.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos dicen que se necesitan más pruebas para entender mejor los efectos a largo plazo y en diferentes condiciones del océano. En 2024 hicieron una prueba más grande frente a la costa de Carolina del Norte, donde colocaron 8,200 toneladas de olivino bajo el agua. Los investigadores seguirán estudiando cómo responde la vida marina y cuánto CO2 se absorbe.

**Frase clave** – Los primeros resultados sugieren que añadir arena de olivino al océano puede ayudar a eliminar CO2 sin dañar a los animales marinos, pero se necesita más investigación para confirmar su seguridad y eficacia.
articles.image_prompt: A serene coastal scene showing a gently curving beach with soft green olivine sand blending into regular sand, with clear shallow waters teeming with diverse small sea creatures like worms, molluscs, and crustaceans visible beneath the surface, all bathed in warm natural light and rendered in delicate, earthy tones of green, beige, and blue.
